The discovery rule tolls the statute of limitations in PFAS cases because the disease typically manifests years or decades after the exposure. The clock starts when the plaintiff knew or should have known of the connection between their illness and the exposure — not when the exposure occurred.

Blood serum PFAS testing is the foundational evidence in individual exposure cases. A serum test showing elevated C8, PFOS, or PFOA levels consistent with the PFAS compounds used in AFFF provides the biological evidence of exposure connecting the plaintiff to the contamination source.

Airport authority documents about known PFAS contamination are among the most valuable evidence in these cases. Many airport authorities conducted internal assessments before any public disclosure. Those internal assessments — frequently withheld in initial discovery responses — establish that the authority knew of the contamination while workers continued to be exposed.

The military's PFAS liability — for AFFF used at airports with military operations or on airport land that was previously military — creates a federal defendant alongside the airport authority and AFFF manufacturer. Federal PFAS claims have different procedural requirements and potentially much larger damage pools.

Bladder cancer and kidney cancer are the two cancers with the strongest scientific linkage to PFAS exposure. Presenting expert testimony specifically on the PFAS-cancer causation science — including the National Academies of Sciences 2022 report — is essential to establishing causation in every cancer case.

Thyroid disease claims in PFAS cases require specific endocrinology expert testimony because the mechanism of PFAS-thyroid disruption differs from cancer causation. The expert testimony must specifically address the dose-response relationship between the plaintiff's measured exposure levels and their specific thyroid diagnosis.

Groundwater monitoring records maintained by the airport authority and by state environmental agencies document the spread of PFAS contamination over time. These records establish which zones had elevated PFAS concentrations at the time the plaintiff was exposed.

AFFF manufacturer 3M, DuPont, and Chemours have been defendants in PFAS litigation nationally, and many cases have been resolved through consolidated multidistrict litigation. Understanding whether your client's claim fits within an existing MDL requires reviewing the MDL settlement terms and exclusions carefully.

Fire training logs are the most direct evidence of exposure frequency and duration. These logs document when AFFF was used, how much, and in what location. Comparing the fire training log to the employment records of airport workers establishes who was present during AFFF exercises and for how long.

Community residents near airports with PFAS contamination have claims against the airport authority for groundwater contamination affecting residential drinking water. These claims typically proceed as mass tort cases with damages that are both property-based and health-based.

The regulatory history of PFAS is itself evidence of the defendants' knowledge. EPA published guidance on PFAS health risks in 2009. FAA issued advisories about PFAS in AFFF in 2015. Airport authorities that continued AFFF training after those dates without implementing alternative methods had regulatory notice of the risk.

Military contractor employees who worked on AFFF systems at joint-use airports have FTCA claims against the federal government alongside state tort claims against the airport authority. These cases require careful jurisdictional analysis.

Immunotoxicity claims — where PFAS exposure caused immune system suppression resulting in more frequent or more severe infections — require immunology expert testimony documenting the dose-response relationship.

Reproductive harm claims — miscarriage, birth defects, reduced fertility — related to PFAS exposure require obstetric and toxicology expert testimony. The epidemiological evidence for reproductive harm from PFAS is stronger than many defendants acknowledge.

Workplace monitoring records — industrial hygiene records, air quality monitoring, and personal exposure monitoring data — are discoverable in airport PFAS cases and frequently show that exposure levels exceeded current EPA health advisory levels.

In cases involving multiple PFAS compounds, the cumulative exposure and combined toxicity of the PFAS mixture must be addressed by the toxicology expert. Current science recognizes that health effects of PFAS mixtures may exceed those of individual compounds.

The statute of limitations defense in PFAS cases is frequently raised by defendants as a motion to dismiss strategy. I file all PFAS cases with extensive allegations about the discovery rule — when the plaintiff first learned of the connection between their illness and the airport PFAS exposure.

Airport PFAS cases involving multiple plaintiffs from the same worker population are candidates for coordination as related cases in the same court, even if not technically a class action. Coordinated discovery allows efficient development of the common evidence.

Environmental justice considerations apply in PFAS cases involving airports in lower-income or minority communities where the affected population had less political power to demand early remediation. Courts and juries have shown increasing receptivity to environmental justice arguments in contamination cases.
lost-delayed-luggage: The Montreal Convention's SDR cap applies to international flights — but not to domestic US flights. For domestic US baggage claims there is no Montreal Convention cap, and the recovery is limited only by provable damages. Many attorneys incorrectly apply the Convention cap to domestic claims.

The property irregularity report filed at the destination airport is the foundation document of every lost luggage case. Without a PIR, the airline will deny the claim entirely. I always verify that a PIR was filed before proceeding.

Airlines calculate replacement value for lost luggage at depreciated value — not replacement cost. For professional equipment, electronics, and business materials, the difference between depreciated value and replacement cost is substantial. I document replacement cost, not depreciated value, and fight the depreciation argument separately.

Essential medication in lost luggage is the highest-priority damages category because the harm is not economic — it is physical. A passenger whose insulin, blood pressure medication, or anti-seizure medication was lost has a medical harm claim measured by the health consequences of the medication interruption, not the value of the medication itself.

Business trip luggage losses — where a passenger was traveling for a specific purpose and their missing luggage caused the business purpose to fail — require documenting the consequential economic damages with specificity. A salesperson who lost product samples and missed a client presentation has consequential business damages that are recoverable if documented contemporaneously.

The Montreal Convention's exoneration defense for baggage damage allows airlines to avoid liability if the inherent defect of the baggage caused the damage. I counter this defense by documenting the condition of the luggage at check-in — photographs taken at the check-in counter of an intact, undamaged bag effectively defeat the inherent defect argument.
In cases involving multiple checked bags where only some were lost, I calculate damages separately for each bag because the airline's liability for each is independent.
The 21-day declaration requirement under the Montreal Convention is a strict deadline. Missing this deadline can bar the Convention claim entirely.

High-value declaration at check-in — the option to declare a higher value and pay a fee — is the mechanism the airline uses to argue the passenger had the means to protect themselves and chose not to. My response: the fee-based excess valuation system does not discharge the airline's duty of care for ordinary baggage handling.

Airline loyalty program status affects baggage handling in practice — premium status bags are handled differently in the operations system. When a high-status passenger's bag was lost and the airline's own operational data shows bags in that category have a lower loss rate, the deviation from that lower rate is evidence of specific negligence.

Photography at check-in is now standard for anyone handling high-value items. I advise clients to photograph the exterior of every bag at the check-in counter, the contents before packing, and the luggage tag attachment.

Baggage tracking data maintained by the airline — showing the last scan location of the bag before it went missing — is discoverable and frequently reveals the bag was misrouted. When the airline cannot explain where the bag went after the last scan, the burden shifts to them to prove they exercised reasonable care.

Wedding attire, custom sports equipment, and irreplaceable sentimental items create damages that exceed monetary replacement value. Courts in several jurisdictions have allowed non-economic damages for the loss of irreplaceable items when the airline's negligence was egregious.

In cases where a lost bag contained original artwork or antiques, the valuation dispute requires expert appraisal testimony. An independent appraiser's report establishing market value for unique items is necessary to overcome the airline's standard replacement cost methodology.

When an airline's own baggage handling procedures were not followed — a procedure deviation documented in the airline's internal audit records — the deviation from the airline's own standard of care is itself negligence.

Delayed baggage that arrived so late that it was unusable for its purpose creates consequential damages not captured in standard replacement value calculations. A wedding dress that arrived three days after the wedding was worth more than its replacement value to its owner.

In cases involving frequent flyers with documented travel histories, the airline's data showing the passenger's luggage had never been lost in hundreds of previous flights makes the specific loss more suspicious and makes the loss harder to attribute to the passenger's handling.

Airlines frequently settle luggage claims with replacement value vouchers rather than cash. Accepting a voucher may require releasing tort claims that exceed the voucher value. I advise clients that vouchers are not equivalent to cash settlements.

Baggage damage from liquid — where an electronics-containing bag was stored in a wet environment or sprayed with de-icing fluid — requires establishing that the liquid exposure occurred while the bag was in the airline's custody. Physical evidence must be documented before the bag is cleaned.

In cases where a passenger's bag was delivered to the wrong person, the airline's chain of custody failure is documented in the delivery records. If the mix-up caused the claimant to be without belongings for an extended period, all consequential damages from that period are recoverable.
rental-car-accidents: Rental car facility accidents involve a property owner (the airport authority), a lessee (the rental car company), and a user (the renter). All three may have liability depending on whether the injury resulted from a facility design failure, equipment failure, or operational failure — often all three simultaneously.

The rental car return lane is the most dangerous zone in any rental car facility. Returning renters unfamiliar with the facility layout are driving into a low-visibility area with multiple other vehicles, exit processing booths, and shuttle bus crossing points all converging in a tight space. The traffic engineering design is the airport authority's responsibility.

Shuttle bus injuries in the transit between the terminal and the rental car facility are among the most underreported rental car facility claims. These shuttles carry multiple passengers with luggage, make frequent stops in traffic, and generate emergency braking injuries, fall-during-entry injuries, and closing-door injuries.

Vehicle maintenance records for rental fleet vehicles are discoverable and frequently reveal that the specific vehicle had known mechanical issues before it was rented. Brake wear, tire condition, and steering component records are most relevant. A vehicle rented with known deficiencies that contributed to an accident is a negligent entrustment case.

In cases where a rental car accident was caused by sudden mechanical failure — a blown tire, brake failure, or steering failure — the product liability chain includes the vehicle manufacturer, the tire manufacturer, and the rental car company as the entity responsible for maintenance.

The loss damage waiver that rental car companies offer is not insurance — it is a waiver of the rental company's right to sue the renter for damage to the rental vehicle. It does not affect the liability of the rental company for injuries to the renter caused by negligence in vehicle maintenance or facility management.

Rental car company employees who direct traffic in the return area are working in the most accident-prone zone. If an employee's direction contributed to a collision — waving a vehicle forward into a path that was not clear — the employee's negligence is directly attributable to the rental company.
In cases involving injuries to pedestrians in rental car lots struck by rental vehicles operated by employees, the rental company is directly liable as the employer under respondeat superior.

Lighting deficiencies in multi-story rental car garages create significant liability for both the airport authority and the rental car operator. Multi-story structures are required to meet specific IES illumination standards. When illumination measurements fall below those standards, the deficiency is quantifiable negligence.

Fueling station injuries in rental car facilities — where a renter is injured while fueling a vehicle before return — involve the rental company's duty to maintain safe fueling areas, including spill prevention and adequate lighting.

In cases involving injuries caused by defective GPS or navigation equipment in rental vehicles — where a driver following incorrect GPS directions was directed into a hazardous situation — the rental company may have liability for providing defective navigation equipment alongside any product liability claim.

Rental car company employee background check practices are discoverable in cases where an employee's conduct was the direct cause of harm. If an employee had a prior driving violation history that the rental company ignored in hiring, the negligent hiring theory supports punitive damages.

Damaged windshield and visibility impairment claims arise when a rental car is provided with a cracked or significantly scratched windshield. If the visibility impairment contributed to an accident, the rental company's failure to identify and address the windshield damage before renting the vehicle is negligence per se.

In cases where the rental car company's roadside assistance program failed to respond within a reasonable time after a breakdown in an unsafe location, the failure of the roadside assistance program is independent negligence.

Pre-existing body damage to rental vehicles is regularly documented at check-in but the documentation process creates liability issues. If the damage documentation log was signed under time pressure and the renter did not notice damage that later contributed to an accident, the rental company's failure to disclose is independently negligent.

Electric vehicle rental injuries present new liability categories as EVs become common in rental fleets. Defective charging equipment, software-related vehicle behavior anomalies, and inadequate driver orientation for EV operation are all new theories.

Airport authority liability in rental car facility injuries extends beyond facility design to the access road and signage leading to the rental car facility. If confusing signage caused a driver to make an unexpected maneuver that contributed to an accident, the signage failure is the authority's direct negligence.

In cases involving injuries to returning renters who were struck by other rental vehicles while walking from their returned vehicle to the shuttle pickup area, the path design is the rental company's and airport authority's shared responsibility.

Third-party visitor injuries in rental car facilities — non-renters who were picking up or dropping off passengers — have the same rights as renters against both the rental car company and the airport authority.

In multiple-vehicle accidents in rental car facilities where fault is disputed, I always request the facility's traffic camera footage immediately. Most modern rental car facilities have comprehensive traffic camera coverage of their vehicle lanes, and that footage resolves disputed-fault claims definitively when preserved before being overwritten.
airline-delay-cancellation-injury: The critical threshold question in delay and cancellation injury cases is whether the event was an extraordinary circumstance under DOT regulations. Weather delays and ATC failures qualify. Maintenance delays, crew scheduling failures, and overbooking do not. Misclassifying a controllable delay as extraordinary is one of the most common airline litigation strategies.

Flight operations records — specifically the airline's Internal Maintenance Log and Operations Control Center records — document the true reason for a delay or cancellation. These records frequently contradict the reason stated to passengers. I request them early in discovery before the airline's litigation team has prepared a narrative around them.

Passengers who suffer physical harm as a direct result of delay conditions — a fall in a crowded gate area, a medical event from extended waiting without adequate accommodation — have tort claims against the airline separate from any DOT compensation rights.

Elderly and disabled passengers who are forced to wait for extended periods without adequate accommodation are the plaintiffs with the strongest delay injury cases because the airline's failure to accommodate them specifically is both a tort and a regulatory violation under 14 CFR Part 382.

In cases where the airline's delay resulted in a medical emergency — a passenger ran out of essential medication because the delay exceeded the medication supply they had — the medical consequences of medication interruption are compensable tort damages if the airline's delay was the proximate cause.

DOT's customer service plan regulations require airlines to provide specific accommodations during tarmac delays exceeding three hours for domestic flights. Failure to provide those accommodations during a tarmac delay creates a regulatory violation that supports a per se negligence argument in any resulting injury case.

Physical altercation injuries during overbooking situations — where an airline gate agent or law enforcement officer used force to remove an overbooked passenger — create both a personal injury claim against the airline and potentially a civil rights claim against the law enforcement officer involved.

In cases where a delay caused a passenger to miss a non-refundable hotel, tour, or event at their destination, the consequential economic damages are recoverable from the airline only if the airline was notified of the special circumstances at the time of booking or at the time the delay was announced.

Tarmac delay food and water deprivation cases — where passengers were confined to a grounded aircraft without adequate food, water, or restroom access — have been successfully pursued. The physical conditions during an extended tarmac confinement are themselves tortious when they cause measurable harm.

The Montreal Convention applies to international flight delays and provides a damage cap for delay damages, but the Convention's delay provisions do not preempt US tort claims for physical injuries caused by delay conditions.

In cases involving missed connections due to the airline's delay — where the missed connection caused the passenger to be stranded at a hub airport for an extended period and the stranding itself caused injury — the airline's responsibility for the stranding is the proximate cause of the injury.

Flight crew rest requirement violations — where an airline operated a flight with crew that exceeded FAA duty time limits, causing a degraded operational decision — create a specific FAA regulatory violation claim alongside the standard negligence theory.

In cases involving delays caused by cybersecurity incidents or IT failures at the airline, the airline cannot claim extraordinary circumstance protection for internally caused technical failures. Operational IT failures are foreseeable and preventable.

Pregnancy complications that resulted from an extended delay — where a pregnant passenger was denied medical accommodation during a delay — create a specific ADA and Rehabilitation Act claim alongside the standard tort claim.

In cases where the airline's app or customer communication system provided incorrect information about the delay — directing passengers to a gate that was subsequently changed — the miscommunication itself is independently negligent if the passenger relied on it to their detriment.

Baggage-related injuries during rebooking chaos — where passengers are suddenly required to collect and re-check bags after a cancellation, creating crowded and hurried conditions — involve the airport authority's premises liability alongside the airline's responsibility for the conditions its cancellation created.

In cases where stranded passengers were denied hotel accommodations by the airline after a cancellation within the airline's control, and who slept in the terminal and suffered physical harm from inadequate sleeping conditions, the airline's refusal to provide accommodations is both a regulatory violation and the proximate cause of the resulting physical harm.

Airlines are increasingly using automated rebooking systems that make rebooking decisions without human review. In cases where an automated rebooking put a passenger in a situation that caused harm — rebooking an unaccompanied minor onto a route without UM service — the automation decision is itself negligent conduct attributable to the airline.

The economic damages from airline delay and cancellation cases are often larger than they appear initially because they include not only direct costs but also consequential business losses, the cost of the traveler's time at professional rates, and the downstream effects of missing a business meeting, family event, or medical appointment.

In cases involving repeated delays of the same aircraft on the same route on the same day — where an aircraft accumulated multiple delays from a morning flight that cascaded through the day — the airline's failure to remove the problematic aircraft from service earlier is itself negligent conduct.
disabled-passenger-assistance: Airline assistance failures for disabled passengers create liability under both the Air Carrier Access Act and state tort law simultaneously. The ACAA creates a federal regulatory framework, but it does not preempt state tort claims in most circuits. I pursue both tracks in parallel.

Airlines are required by DOT regulations to provide wheelchair assistance to any passenger who requests it, regardless of the reason for the request. The failure to provide requested assistance, or the provision of inadequate assistance that results in injury, is both a regulatory violation and a tort.

Wheelchair transfer injuries — where a passenger is injured during a transfer from their personal wheelchair to an aisle chair or airport wheelchair — are among the most common disabled passenger injury claims. The airline's wheelchair contractor is the primary defendant, but the airline is responsible for the contractor's conduct under the ACAA.

Aisle chair equipment maintenance is a specific evidence target in transfer injury cases. Aisle chairs that are unstable, have worn foot supports, or have malfunctioning restraints create a foreseeable transfer injury risk. The maintenance records for the specific aisle chair used are documents I request on day one.

Motorized scooter damage during transport — particularly lithium battery damage that renders the device inoperable — creates both property damage and ADA accommodation claims. An immobile passenger who cannot reach their destination accommodation because their mobility device was damaged is a scenario that courts have valued substantially.

Jet bridge gap injuries to wheelchair users — where the gap between the jet bridge and the aircraft door threshold is too wide for safe wheelchair transfer — involve both the airline and the airport authority. The airport authority controls jet bridge positioning standards; the airline controls the aircraft parking position.

In cases where a passenger's request for wheelchair assistance was logged but the assistance was never provided — resulting in an injury when the passenger attempted to navigate the terminal independently — the logged request is the most powerful evidence in the case.

Connecting flight scenarios create enhanced disability assistance obligations. An airline that knows a passenger requires assistance and has a tight connection has an obligation to coordinate assistance for the connection, not just the origin or destination.

Visual impairment assistance failures are a less frequently litigated but significant category. Visually impaired passengers who are not provided the assistance they requested and are injured have the same legal remedies as mobility-impaired passengers under the ACAA.

Improper restraint in wheelchair transport — too loose, too tight, or positioned incorrectly for the passenger's specific disability — creates an injury risk during the transport phase. The wheelchair contractor's training protocols for restraint application are discoverable and are frequently found inadequate.

In cases involving falls from moving wheelchairs in the terminal, the speed of transport and the condition of the floor surface are both relevant. A wheelchair pusher who was moving at a speed unsafe for the passenger's condition is directly negligent.

In cases where a passenger with a known medical condition was denied assistance and the denial worsened the condition, the ACAA violation and the medical negligence are distinct theories that must be developed separately.

Post-September 11 security procedures have created specific conflict points for disabled passengers — enhanced screening, removal of assistive devices, isolation from accompanying family members. When security screening procedures deviate from TSA's own disability accommodation SOPs and result in injury, the FTCA claim is available alongside the ACAA claim.

Passengers who are required to gate-check their mobility devices have specific rights under DOT regulations about the condition in which the device must be returned. Devices returned damaged or with batteries disconnected improperly are ACAA violations with both property and consequential damages.

In cases involving oxygen-dependent passengers, the airline's obligation to accommodate in-flight oxygen use extends to the assistance provided in the terminal. A passenger who required oxygen and was not provided assistance in navigating the terminal — resulting in exertion that caused a medical event — has a combined ACAA violation and medical emergency negligence claim.

The standard of care for airlines under the ACAA is defined in DOT's 14 CFR Part 382, which contains specific, quantitative requirements. I compare the airline's conduct against the specific regulatory requirements — not just the general duty of care — to establish regulatory violation as the negligence standard.

International disabled passenger assistance claims combine ACAA requirements with Montreal Convention considerations for injuries during international embarkation and disembarkation. The intersection of these two frameworks requires careful analysis.

In cases where a disabled passenger was accommodated on an initial flight but assistance was not communicated to the connecting carrier, the initial carrier is responsible for the communication failure. DOT regulations require the initial carrier to transmit passenger assistance information to all subsequent carriers.
Class action considerations in disabled passenger assistance cases arise when an airline has a systemic policy of inadequate accommodation that affects a defined class of disabled passengers.

The ACAA does not have a private right of action for damages in all circuits — some courts have held that ACAA violations must be pursued through DOT complaints. In those circuits, the state tort claim becomes the primary damages vehicle and the ACAA violation is evidence of negligence per se.
international-travel-claims: The Montreal Convention applies to any bodily injury that occurs on board the aircraft or in the course of any operations of embarking or disembarking. Whether a specific injury is covered by the Convention — and therefore subject to its strict liability and damage cap provisions — is the threshold question in every international travel injury case.

Article 17 of the Montreal Convention creates strict liability for airlines for passenger bodily injury up to approximately 128,821 Special Drawing Rights (about $175,000 USD at current rates). Above that amount, the passenger must prove the airline was negligent. Understanding the two-tier liability structure is essential to accurate case valuation.
The Montreal Convention's accident requirement for Article 17 claims has been interpreted broadly by US courts to include unexpected and unusual events outside the normal flight operation.

The two-year limitation period under the Montreal Convention is a hard deadline that no court can extend. I calendar this deadline in every international travel injury case and file before it expires regardless of the status of any settlement negotiations.

The exoneration defense under Article 21 allows airlines to avoid liability above the SDR threshold by proving the injury was solely caused by negligence of the claimant or by the actions of a third party. The burden of establishing this defense is on the airline.

Jurisdiction for Montreal Convention claims is available in any of five places under Article 33: the carrier's domicile, the carrier's principal place of business, the place of business where the ticket was purchased, the place of destination, or the passenger's principal and permanent residence for bodily injury claims.

Injuries during international transit that occur in the terminal between flights present a unique jurisdictional question: does the Montreal Convention apply to the terminal phase, or only to the aircraft operations?

Baggage damage claims under the Montreal Convention are capped at 1,288 SDR per passenger unless the passenger made a special declaration of value at check-in. Most passengers do not make this declaration.

Mental injury claims under the Montreal Convention for pure psychological injury without accompanying physical injury have been inconsistently treated by US courts. The general rule is that pure mental distress without physical injury is not recoverable under the Convention.

Code-share flights create specific liability allocation questions under the Montreal Convention. When a ticket is sold by one carrier but operated by another, both the marketing carrier and the operating carrier may have liability.

In cases where the injury occurred on a non-US carrier during an international flight arriving in the US, the carrier may attempt to apply a different national court. The Montreal Convention's jurisdictional framework typically allows the case to proceed in US court if the passenger's principal and permanent residence is in the US.
Dangerous goods incidents on international flights — where improperly declared or improperly packaged cargo caused an injury — involve both the shipper and the airline as defendants.

In cases involving injuries sustained during emergency evacuations of international flights, the Montreal Convention applies to the evacuation itself, but the adequacy of the evacuation equipment and procedures may also involve US regulatory claims under FAA regulations.

Crew negligence claims during international flights have a specific analysis under the Montreal Convention: the airline is strictly liable for crew negligence up to the SDR threshold, and the plaintiff need not prove fault below that threshold.

Turbulence injury cases during international flights require a careful investigation of whether the turbulence was foreseeable based on the flight's route and weather forecasts. If the airline had access to meteorological information showing significant turbulence and failed to instruct passengers to fasten seatbelts, the failure is an accident under the Convention.
In cases involving passenger death on an international flight, the Montreal Convention creates a strict liability claim for the surviving family members up to the SDR threshold.

US courts have held that the Montreal Convention preempts state law claims for the same injury on international flights. This means a passenger injured on an international flight cannot pursue a state law negligence claim alongside a Convention claim. However, claims against non-carrier third parties are not preempted.

Medical diversions during international flights create combined airline liability for the adequacy of the in-flight medical response and hospital liability for the treatment received after diversion. The Montreal Convention covers the airline's in-flight conduct; the hospital's conduct is governed by the law of the country where the diversion occurred.

Overbooking-related injuries — physical altercations during involuntary bumping — have been the subject of significant litigation. The airline cannot invoke the overbooking defense to avoid Montreal Convention liability for physical injury.

In cases involving passengers injured on international flights who are not US citizens, the principal and permanent residence jurisdictional basis under Article 33(2) does not apply. Those plaintiffs must rely on one of the other four jurisdictional bases under Article 33(1).
child-unaccompanied-minor: Unaccompanied minor programs at airlines create specific, documented duties that are defined in the airline's own UM policies and in DOT regulations. When an airline accepts a child as an unaccompanied minor, it assumes custodial responsibility for that child's safety from check-in to hand-off to the authorized recipient at the destination.

The airline's UM documentation — the UM form signed at check-in, the custody transfer log, and the flight record — establishes the chain of responsibility. Any gap in this documentation is evidence of an operational failure that allowed the harm to occur.

In cases where an unaccompanied minor was lost in an airport between connecting flights, I obtain the complete flight operations record to establish who had custody of the child at each transfer point and what specifically went wrong in the hand-off process.

Airline UM programs require fee payment, and that fee creates a heightened duty of care beyond the general carrier obligation. The airline charged specifically for the supervision service and represented to the parents that qualified supervision would be provided throughout the journey.

Physical harm to unaccompanied minors during airport transits — falls, injuries caused by inadequate supervision, or injuries from airport hazards — involve both the airline for supervision failure and the airport authority for the hazardous condition.

Psychological harm to unaccompanied minors from lost-in-airport experiences is compensable damage that requires child psychology expert testimony to quantify. The trauma of being separated from adults in an unfamiliar airport environment can produce lasting anxiety symptoms.

In cases involving the release of an unaccompanied minor to an unauthorized person — a non-designated adult who was able to claim the child at the destination — the airline's identity verification failure is direct negligence. The UM policy requires specific identification verification at pick-up.

Connecting flight failures in UM programs — where the airline failed to ensure the child made their connection — involve the airline's duty to monitor the child's progress through the itinerary. This monitoring obligation does not end at the departure gate of the first flight.

In cases involving harm to unaccompanied minors caused by airline employees, the airline's vicarious liability is direct. The airline cannot claim the harm was outside the scope of its duty simply because it involved an employee rather than a facility hazard.

Airlines frequently attempt to invoke the Montreal Convention's damage caps for unaccompanied minor injury cases on international flights. However, whether the Convention applies depends on whether the injury meets the Article 17 accident definition. Harms caused by airline supervision failures may not meet that definition.

Regulatory requirements for UM programs are contained in FAA regulations and DOT guidance. I identify every specific regulatory requirement that was violated and use those violations as the negligence standard in the case.

In cases where an unaccompanied minor experienced a medical emergency that the airline's staff failed to recognize or address, the combined UM supervision failure and medical emergency negligence creates two independent liability theories.

Airport authority liability in UM cases arises when the airport's facilities contributed to the harm. If an unaccompanied minor was injured because an unsupervised child accessed an unsafe area — a construction zone or restricted area — both the airline and the airport authority are defendants.

Family separation trauma damages in UM cases extend beyond the child to the parents who were left waiting at the destination for a child who did not arrive. Parent anxiety, emergency travel costs, and the emotional impact of believing their child was in danger are compensable damages.

In cases involving minors who are dual citizens or who travel on international routes, the jurisdiction question requires careful analysis of whether the Montreal Convention applies and whether the liability framework under the Convention benefits or harms the recovery.

The age of the unaccompanied minor matters to the duty of care analysis. The airline's obligations for a 5-year-old traveling alone are significantly more intensive than for a 15-year-old. Failures within each age tier must be evaluated against the standards specific to that age group.

In wrongful death cases involving unaccompanied minors, the damages include not only the economic loss of the child's future earning capacity but also the profound non-economic loss of the child's life — damages that courts and juries assess with particular severity.

Negligent hiring claims in UM cases arise when the airline employee responsible for the minor had a background that should have disqualified them from UM supervision duties. If background check records show the employee had prior incidents involving children, the negligent hiring theory adds punitive damages exposure.

Post-incident counseling and psychological treatment for the minor are both compensable damages and evidence of the psychological harm caused by the airline's breach. Therapy records from a licensed child psychologist are among the strongest evidence of non-economic harm.

In cases involving multiple UM failures by the same airline — where prior UM program failures are discoverable through public records or prior litigation — the pattern establishes that the airline's UM program was systemically inadequate and that management knew of the deficiency.
slip-fall-wet-weather: Wet weather slip cases require weather documentation that standard slip cases do not need. I obtain NOAA hourly precipitation records for the specific airport on the incident date, timestamped to establish exactly when precipitation was occurring and for how long before the fall.

The precipitation onset-to-treatment gap is the central liability issue in wet weather slip cases. If rain began at 2:00 PM and the victim fell at 2:45 PM, the question is whether 45 minutes was adequate time for the airport authority and its cleaning contractors to respond. Industry standards define reasonable response times.

Wet floor mat placement protocols are documented in most major airport cleaning contracts. If the contract requires wet floor mats at all exterior entry points during precipitation and the mats were not placed when the victim fell, the contractor violated its own contractual obligations.

De-icing runoff creates secondary interior wet floor hazards during and after snowstorms. De-icing fluid applied to the curbside area tracks into the terminal, creating wet and potentially slippery floor conditions that are chemically different from rain-tracked moisture.

Footwear seasonality is particularly relevant in wet weather cases. The inappropriate footwear comparative fault defense is stronger in wet weather cases because the precipitation was publicly known. My response: reasonable air travelers wear seasonally appropriate footwear, and seasonal footwear is not a legal bar to recovery.

Snow removal contract compliance is a specific evidence target in winter weather slip cases. The airport's snow removal contractor has a contract that defines response times, removal standards, and areas of responsibility. Contract compliance records showing whether the contractor met its obligations before the incident are key evidence.

Drainage design failures create recurring wet weather hazard conditions. If a specific terminal entry has poor drainage that causes water to pool at the threshold during rain events, and prior incidents have occurred at the same location during rain, the drainage design failure establishes long-term constructive notice.

In cases involving ice that formed from refreezing of snowmelt — where water that melted during the day refroze overnight and created black ice — the overnight temperature records are as important as the daytime precipitation records. The airport authority had an obligation to monitor and treat refreezing conditions.

Wet weather slip injuries that result from tracking moisture from outdoor areas into indoor zones place a higher duty on the airport authority because the moisture source is known and predictable. Unlike transient spills, precipitation-caused floor wetness is foreseeable from the moment rain begins.

Security screening areas present a unique wet weather hazard because passengers must remove shoes during the screening process and then walk in stocking feet on surfaces that are frequently wet from passenger foot traffic tracking in from outside.

In winter weather cases involving elderly passengers, the airport authority's obligation to provide wheelchair assistance or special accommodation for passengers navigating icy outdoor surfaces is relevant. If an elderly passenger was injured on icy curbside while no assistance was offered, the failure to proactively offer accommodation is a factor in the liability analysis.

The specific floor surface material and its wet-weather performance characteristics are technical evidence in any wet weather slip case. Polished terrazzo has a dramatically lower wet coefficient of friction than brushed concrete or rubber mat surfaces.

Umbrella storage facilities at terminal entries are a telling piece of evidence. If the airport installed umbrella stands, they acknowledged that wet umbrellas were a floor hazard. If they installed umbrella stands but did not provide floor mats, they addressed one form of the hazard while ignoring a larger one.

In cases involving falls at outdoor aircraft boarding areas during precipitation, the airline's decision to proceed with outdoor boarding rather than waiting for conditions to improve or providing covered access is an independent operational negligence.

Wet weather claims at airports with covered walkways and jet bridges present a comparative context. If the airport has jet bridge boarding at most gates but the victim's gate used exposed boarding stairs, the airport authority's decision to use that gate configuration for that flight is relevant.

Squeegee and drainage crew deployment records are a specific evidence category in major wet weather events. Some airports deploy squeegee crews to manage floor wetness during heavy precipitation. If the airport had a wet floor management protocol that required squeegee crews and those crews were not deployed, the protocol failure is documented negligence.

In cases where the fall occurred immediately after the passenger exited a public restroom — where the restroom drainage caused water to track into the adjacent corridor — the restroom maintenance contractor is a separate defendant from the general terminal maintenance contractor.

At coastal airports during tropical storm events, the airport authority often makes the decision to remain open rather than close, exposing passengers to hazardous conditions. The decision to remain open during severe weather expands the duty of care to passengers navigating the facility.

Wet weather slip cases in states with contributory negligence rules require particular attention to the victim's awareness of the weather conditions. I always document what information was available to the passenger about weather conditions at the time of the fall.

Prior wet weather incident reports from the same terminal area are among the most valuable records because they establish that the specific combination of weather condition and floor surface at that location had caused injuries before. The airport authority cannot claim they were unaware of the risk when they have their own incident reports documenting it.
retail-shop-injuries: Airport retail shop injury cases follow the same operator-specific liability analysis as food court cases. The retail concessionaire is the primary defendant for injuries within their leased space; the airport authority is liable for common areas and for the concessionaire's lease compliance.

Slip and fall injuries in airport retail shops frequently involve merchandise displays that obscure floor hazards — clothing racks that hide wet spots, product display tables that block visibility of floor transitions. The display configuration is the retailer's choice, and the hazard created by that configuration is the retailer's liability.

In cases involving merchandise that fell from display shelving and struck a customer, the product display design and the shelf load limits are the technical evidence targets. If the shelving was loaded beyond its rated capacity, or if the display configuration was unstable, the retailer's negligent display practices are the basis of liability.

Airport retail leases define who is responsible for floor maintenance within the leased space. In most leases, the concessionaire is responsible for floor maintenance inside the retail area, but the airport authority is responsible for the area immediately outside the shop entrance — the transition zone where many retail shop falls occur.

Retail shop display fixture injuries — where a customer is struck by a falling display fixture, a collapsing clothing rack, or a tipping product stand — involve the retailer as the primary defendant and potentially the fixture manufacturer as a product liability defendant if the fixture was defective.

In cases involving slip and fall injuries from liquid tracked from adjacent food and beverage concessions into retail areas, the liquid source creates liability against the food and beverage operator. If the retail area was consistently receiving tracked moisture from an adjacent food court, both the food court operator and the retail operator may share liability.

Retail shop queue management failures — where a crowded checkout line extended into the store's main walking area, creating a trip hazard — are the retailer's operational responsibility. If the retailer failed to manage the checkout queue despite knowing the store was overcrowded, the failure is independent negligence.

In cases involving injuries at airport duty-free shops on the international side of security, the duty-free operator is the primary defendant. Duty-free operators frequently operate under different lease terms than domestic concessionaires, and their lease structure may include different indemnification obligations.

High-value product displays in airport retail shops — luxury goods, electronics, watches — frequently use pedestal displays that create trip hazards when positioned in walking paths. The positioning of these displays is an operational decision by the retailer that creates foreseeable injury risk.

Slip hazards from sample products in airport retail shops — fragrance testers, cosmetic samples, food samples — are the retailer's direct responsibility. A fragrance tester bottle broken on the floor, or a food sample that was dropped in the aisle, creates a retailer liability if the hazard was present long enough that a reasonable inspection would have caught it.

In cases involving injuries from shopping bags that other customers left in walking paths, the retailer has a duty to manage the walkway conditions in their leased space. A store that allowed shopping bags to accumulate in a high-traffic path without directing customers to bag storage or clearing the path is negligent.

Airport retail shop hours extend into late night, when staffing is reduced. Many retail shop slip and fall cases involve late-night incidents when the cleaning schedule was sparse and the staff too thin to monitor floor conditions in the shop. The reduced staffing creates a heightened duty to maintain floor conditions because each individual staff member was responsible for a larger area.

In cases involving children injured in airport retail shops — particularly in toy stores or shops with floor-level displays — the children's level visibility and reach create a specific duty of care argument. Display configurations that are safe for adults may create foreseeable injury risks for children who reach for items at their eye level.

Broken glass injuries in airport wine and spirits shops create a specific product handling liability. If wine bottles were stored improperly and fell, or if a shelf failed under the weight of the product load, the retailer's product storage practices are the basis of liability.

In cases involving injuries from electronic display kiosks or interactive product demonstrations in airport retail shops, the kiosk design and placement are both relevant. Kiosks positioned in high-traffic areas without adequate clearance create foreseeable collision and trip hazards for which the retailer is liable.

Security tag removal devices used in airport retail shops — handheld devices and stationary systems — can create injury risks when used improperly by undertrained staff. If a security tag removal device caused a cut or impact injury, the retailer's staff training records are discoverable evidence.

Airport retail shops that sell heavy merchandise — luggage, electronics, sports equipment — have a heightened duty of care in helping customers carry their purchases to checkout or to their gate. A store clerk who allowed an elderly customer to carry an unreasonably heavy item without offering assistance may have contributed to a fall or strain injury.

In cases involving injuries from revolving door malfunction at airport retail shop entrances, the door maintenance contractor and the retailer are both potential defendants. The revolving door is the retailer's equipment, maintained by a separate contractor. Both carry independent liability for its safe operation.

Retail shop injury cases in airport international terminals often involve different applicable law than domestic terminal cases. If the injury occurred after international check-in and before boarding on an international flight, the question of whether the Montreal Convention applies to the injury should be analyzed before determining the primary legal theory.

The airport authority's right to inspect and enforce lease compliance gives the authority independent awareness of retail shop conditions. If the authority's inspection records show prior lease compliance violations related to floor maintenance or display safety, the authority's knowledge of those violations creates independent liability when a customer is subsequently injured.
jet-bridge-boarding: Outdoor boarding stair cases require investigating the weather conditions at the specific time of boarding or deplaning. Temperature, precipitation, and wind conditions at the exact time — obtainable from NOAA records — establish whether the conditions created a foreseeable risk.

Portable boarding stairs are owned and operated by the ground handler, not the airline. The maintenance records for the specific stair unit including the last inspection date and any reported defects are the first evidence I request in every jet bridge and boarding stair case.

Anti-slip treads on boarding stairs degrade from repeated use and weather exposure. If the treads were worn past the replacement standard at the time of the injury, the ground handler was in breach of its own maintenance protocol.

Handrail adequacy on portable boarding stairs is governed by FAA Advisory Circular standards. A boarding stair unit with handrails that do not meet the height or continuity standards in the Advisory Circular creates per se negligence in states that treat regulatory violation as negligence per se.

The airline has an independent duty of care for passengers during the boarding and deplaning process. If an airline agent observed icy or wet boarding stairs and failed to delay boarding or arrange an alternative boarding method, the airline shares direct liability.

In international airports, passengers injured on boarding stairs may have claims under both state law against the ground handler and the Montreal Convention against the airline. Whether a boarding stair injury meets the Montreal Convention accident definition has been litigated extensively.

Jet bridge malfunction injuries — where a jet bridge moves unexpectedly, gaps between the bridge and aircraft door create a step hazard, or the bridge floor surface is in inadequate condition — involve the jet bridge maintenance contractor as a primary defendant alongside the airline.

The gap between the boarding stair and the aircraft door threshold is a documented injury zone. When the gap is wider than applicable standards, or when the stair unit is positioned at an angle creating an uneven step at the threshold, the positioning decision was made by the ground handler's equipment operator.

In cases involving falls on rain-slicked jet bridge walkways, the bridge surface material and its maintenance condition are central evidence. Many jet bridges use vinyl flooring that becomes slippery when wet. Prior knowledge of the slip hazard and failure to treat it is constructive notice.
Wheelchair-assistance failures at boarding stairs create overlapping theories: negligent assistance, premises liability, and ADA accommodation failure. I pursue all three simultaneously.
Medical complications arising from a fall during deplaning are subject to both the Montreal Convention for international flights and state tort law for domestic flights.

Ramp access injuries at smaller airports — where passengers walk across the tarmac to board via stairs — involve weather exposure and tarmac surface conditions that do not exist in jet bridge boarding situations.

In cases where the boarding stair incident was preceded by a flight delay that left passengers standing on the stairs in deteriorating weather, the airline's decision to continue boarding rather than return passengers to the terminal supports liability.

Night boarding at airports without adequate tarmac lighting creates a separate lighting liability claim. FAA lighting standards for aircraft areas specify minimum illumination levels. When illumination falls below those standards, the regulatory violation creates a per se negligence argument.

In cases where multiple passengers fell at the same boarding gate on the same day due to the same hazard, each incident strengthens the others. Multiple incidents mean the defendant had actual notice after the first one.

The documentation of a boarding stair incident is often incomplete because many injuries do not become apparent until hours or days after the flight. I always instruct clients to photograph the boarding stairs and any visible hazard immediately.

Jet bridge door seal failures that create a step threshold at the aircraft door entrance are a joint-liability situation involving both the airline's ground operations and the airport's jet bridge contractor.

Cold weather operations specifically require anti-icing treatment of boarding surfaces before boarding begins. FAA guidance specifies when anti-icing is required. If boarding proceeded without required anti-icing treatment, the airline's operational decision to proceed was an independent basis for liability.

Boarding stairs at commuter aircraft are smaller, steeper, and less stable than stairs used for mainline aircraft. The specific duty of care for commuter aircraft boarding includes enhanced stability requirements and enhanced supervision of the boarding process.

In cases where the airline's ground operations deviated from the procedures specified in their own Ground Operations Manual for boarding stair positioning and safety, that deviation from the airline's own documented standard of care is straightforward negligence.
shuttle-bus-ground-transportation: Shuttle bus accident cases at airports involve a completely different defendant than terminal injury cases. The shuttle operator carries its own commercial general liability and commercial auto coverage — separate from both the airport authority and the airline. Identifying the specific shuttle operator and pulling their DOT registration on day one is critical.

The airport authority typically contracts with shuttle operators for specific routes — between terminals, between parking and the terminal, between the rental car facility and the terminal. That contract defines the safety standards the operator must meet and gives the authority supervisory responsibility over the operator's performance.

Emergency braking injuries on airport shuttles are among the most common shuttle injury types. A standing passenger who is thrown forward during sudden braking sustains a specific injury pattern — shoulder, wrist, and head impacts — that is documented in the shuttle's dashcam footage and the driver's incident report.

GPS and telematics data from airport shuttle buses logs speed, braking events, and route data. This data has a 30-90 day retention window. A preservation demand must go to the shuttle operator on day one in every shuttle injury case.

In cases involving injuries from shuttle bus doors — closing on a passenger, striking a passenger who was partially in the doorway — the door safety mechanism is the mechanical evidence target. Door edge sensors are required on commercial buses under FMCSA regulations. A door that closed without properly detecting the obstruction is a mechanical violation.

The shuttle route and stop design is the airport authority's responsibility. If the shuttle stops in a location that requires passengers to step down onto uneven ground, cross active vehicle lanes, or navigate without adequate lighting, the stop design failure is the authority's direct liability.

Standing passenger injuries on airport shuttles create a specific negligence analysis because commercial vehicle operators have a heightened duty of care to standing passengers. A bus driver who brakes suddenly without warning when standing passengers are on board is negligent regardless of whether the braking was caused by traffic conditions.

In cases involving injuries during shuttle loading — where passengers were injured while boarding or while their luggage was being loaded — the shuttle driver and the shuttle operator are the primary defendants. The driver's duty to ensure all passengers are safely on board before departing is a specific, documented obligation.

Shuttle bus accident cases involving injured passengers who were connecting to a flight often involve consequential damages — missed flight, rebooking costs, hotel — in addition to the personal injury damages. These consequential damages are recoverable if the shuttle operator's negligence was the direct cause.

The specific shuttle route — whether it traveled on public roads between off-site parking and the terminal, or entirely within the airport property — determines whether the accident is governed by state traffic law, airport operations rules, or both.

In cases where the shuttle bus accident involved a collision with another vehicle, the shuttle operator and the other vehicle's driver are both defendants. The airport authority may share liability if the collision occurred in a zone where the authority controlled traffic flow.

Shuttle bus maintenance records are discoverable in every shuttle injury case. Commercial bus operators are required to maintain Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration-compliant maintenance records. A shuttle that was operated with known mechanical deficiencies is a negligent entrustment case against the operator.

In cases involving injuries to elderly passengers who fell on shuttle buses, the heightened duty of care to elderly passengers includes ensuring they are seated or securely holding a handrail before the bus moves. A driver who departed before an elderly passenger was secured is directly negligent.

Luggage handling on airport shuttles — where drivers or porter staff load and secure luggage in the luggage bay — creates a separate liability when improperly secured luggage shifts or falls and injures passengers. The driver's responsibility to secure luggage before departure is a documented commercial bus operation requirement.

Hotel shuttle injuries from airport hotels involve a fourth category of defendants beyond the airport authority, airline, and terminal operators. Hotel shuttle injuries are primarily the hotel's liability and the shuttle operator's liability — the airport authority is less involved unless the shuttle was operating in an authority-controlled zone.

In cases where the shuttle bus lacked adequate grab handles, seat spacing, or non-slip flooring for the passenger load it was carrying, the equipment inadequacy is the operator's liability. Commercial bus interior specifications include safety requirements for standing passenger capacity and equipment.

Shuttle bus driver fatigue claims require obtaining the driver's hours-of-service records. FMCSA regulations define maximum driving hours for commercial vehicle operators. If the driver who caused the accident had exceeded their hours-of-service limits, the hours violation is evidence of negligent supervision by the operator.

In cases involving children injured on airport shuttle buses — particularly when children were not provided adequate seating or restraint options — the shuttle operator's duty to accommodate child passengers creates specific liability beyond the general duty of care.

Accessibility failures on airport shuttle buses — where a passenger with a mobility disability was not provided accessible boarding or seating — create ADA claims alongside standard tort claims. Commercial bus operators are required to provide accessible transportation under the ADA.

The shuttle operator's insurance coverage limits are the practical ceiling on recovery in most shuttle bus injury cases. I verify the operator's commercial auto and general liability coverage limits through the DOT registration records before assessing the full value of the claim.
